content: Is Haskell-style function application valid CGL code? The author's solution outputs "f123a" for the input "func 123 var".

Hi
Took me a bit longer than the regular timeout to pass the tests without the zeros, and 5 more mins with them. I am not fast and not used to these exercises but from what I've seen so far this one definitely lines with the longer ones.

Constraints
* Please specify the lower and upper bounds for the length of each line in the original CGL code. (The lower bound should be 1 if cases with empty lines won't be added.)

* Please state that any character that is not an English letter will never be part of a variable name. (Example: digits. Underscores would also fall into this category, although they aren't used in the current cases.)

* The last constraint may need to be reworded to avoid confusion, since floating-point numbers appear in the cases.

Cases
* Consider adding cases where the code contains floating-point numbers with or without a sign (positive or negative) and trailing zeros either immediately after the decimal point or later in the fractional part, e.g. -620000.00, 3000.400, +95.1000, -8000.007000.

Thanks.

Oops, I didn't mean to introduce floating-point numbers. I have reworked the test cases accordingly to ensure that all numbers are 32-bit integers.

As for non-alphabetical characters not being part of variable names, do you think that the Goal section covers this sufficiently? The wording I used is "Variables in CGL only consist of English letters (A-Z, a-z)". Thank you!

Hey @kozuechan, thanks for the catch! In CGL terms, "func" will be treated as a variable and hence shortened to "f". However, my code incorrectly handled subsequent variable names if the preceeding variable name is not followed with a non-alphanumeric non-whitespace symbol. I've edited my solution accordingly!

I do plan to add a test case on this. Do you have a sample Haskell-style code snippet I could borrow? Thank you!

Thank you for the explanation. How about this snippet?

lcg = \a -> \m -> \seed -> (a * seed + 327) % m
param = 1000
r = lcg 201 param
::<< (r 42)

Please feel free to rewrite it however you like.
I've added two new test cases covering this scenario :) Do let me know if I'm missing anything
Thanks for the feedback @k4rl. How would you rate the difficulty of the clash?

It's the clash that took me the most time so far (630 participations) safe maybe for a labyrinth one.
The statement is clear and the exercise interesting, so it deserves more feedback from others.
I've updated the test cases; your sample code might fail on them. Do try them again if you have the time and let me know what you think :)

Ah yes, I remember the Goal section mentioning that; I was just distracted by the related constraint. It might be a little less confusing if that constraint is merged into the Goal section (since it's also part of the CGL language specification), but I'm also fine with the current version.

Constraints / Cases
* Consider removing the second-to-last constraint and modifying/adding cases that include a number whose standard notation doesn't shorten the code and therefore remains unchanged. (This won't significantly change the task complexity, I think?)

Cases
* Validator 4 still contains a floating-point number (1.2).

* Validator 4 seems to be the only case that includes some 2-digit multiples of 10. Please modify Test 4 to include such integers.

* Consider modifying/adding cases to include integers with more than 9 trailing zeros.

* Consider modifying/adding cases to include negative integers (using the unary "-" instead of subtraction), since they aren't prohibited under the current constraints.

Thanks.

Sidenote: I'm also not sure how I should classify 70% and 50% in Validator 4. From a CGL perspective, 70 and 50 are integers, but mathematically 70% and 50% represent floating-point values 😆 … I just looked up whether "%" (percent, not modulus) is considered an operator in Excel, and it actually is! One can even write "=A1%", which is equivalent to "=A1/100". Very interesting. Fortunately, such notations are used in (the hidden) Validator 4 only, so it shouldn't cause any confusion about whether the case contradicts the final constraint.

Have made some changes accordingly:

- Removed the floating point number in Validator 4
- Modified Test/Validator 5 to include negative numbers
- Modified Test 6 to include two-digit multiples of 10

The constraint regarding the length of the standard notation vs original number was put in place so that the "shortest code" is undisputedly clear. I didn't want to specify another rule to always keep/convert numbers with 2 trailing zeros as this rule is hidden from Reverse mode clashers.

As for including numbers larger than 1e10, such numbers will exceed the 32-bit limit. Should I raise the limit such that such numbers can be worked into the test cases?

The changes look fine.

> The constraint regarding the length of the standard notation vs original number

You don't need to specify another rule; the existing rule already covers this clearly: convert the number only if the resulting notation is shorter. Because of that, I don't see any issue with removing the constraint and including numbers where the converted form ends up being the same length as the original (and therefore shouldn't be converted).

For Reverse mode, having both conversion and non-conversion instances within the same test case should give players a strong hint about when conversion is expected. An example such as x = 1 + 10 + 100 + 1000 + 10000 (or a slightly less obvious variant, if you prefer) would naturally demonstrate the pattern.

> As for including numbers larger than 1e10

You're right. Please ignore my earlier suggestion.

Thanks.
